english: Enter the world of German taxes and finance. Learn the key financial words. Understand how the German tax system shapes daily life.
Learn 18 core words from tax and finance
Understand Umsatz, Gewinn, Verlust, and Kredit
Experience Verumius's first tax consultation

The tax advisor's office is immaculate. Framed certificates line the wall. A desktop shows tax software with columns of numbers. Verumius sits across, his folder of crumpled receipts looking embarrassingly casual next to the advisor's color-coded filing system.
Good day, Mr Verumius. Let us discuss your tax situation. Since you work freelance, we need to clarify a few points.
I brought all the receipts. I hope that is enough?
We will take a look. By the end of the month you will have submitted your tax return. No worries.
levy, tax, submission
Ab (off/away) + Gabe (giving). Both the act of handing in\nand the money you hand to the state.

A: Which levies do I have to pay?
B: Social contributions and taxes.
A: How high are the levies in total?
B: About 40 percent of gross wages.

'Ab' (off/away) + 'Gabe' (giving). A levy, a tax, or a submission. Die Abgabe covers both the act of handing something in and the money you hand over to the state. 'Sozialabgaben' (social contributions) eat roughly 40% of German gross income.
revenue, turnover
Um (around) + Satz (rate/set). What comes in before costs.\n'Umsatzsteuer' (VAT) is 19% on almost everything.

A: How high was your revenue last year?
B: About 80,000 euros.
A: And after deducting costs?
B: Then the profit remains.

'Um' (around) + 'Satz' (rate/set). Revenue, turnover. Der Umsatz is what comes in before costs. 'Umsatzsteuer' (sales tax/VAT) is Germany's 19% on almost everything. The word literally means 'conversion rate' of goods to money.
profit
From 'gewinnen' (to win/gain). What remains after all costs.\n'Gewinn und Verlust' is the fundamental equation.

A: Did the company make a profit?
B: Yes, the profit was higher than expected.
A: How do you calculate profit?
B: Revenue minus costs equals profit.

From 'gewinnen' (to win/gain). Profit. Der Gewinn is what remains after all costs. 'Gewinn und Verlust' (profit and loss) is the fundamental equation of every business. Winning in business is literally called Gewinn.
loss
From 'verlieren' (to lose). The opposite of Gewinn.\n'Verluste machen' is every entrepreneur's nightmare.

A: Did the company make a loss?
B: Unfortunately yes. The loss was considerable.
A: Can you deduct losses from taxes?
B: Yes, losses reduce the tax burden.

From 'verlieren' (to lose). Loss. Der Verlust. The opposite of Gewinn. 'Verluste machen' (to make losses) is every entrepreneur's nightmare. The -ust ending comes from the old past participle of verlieren.
debts
From 'Schuld' (guilt/debt). Always plural for money owed.\nIn German, debt and guilt share the same root.

A: Does he have debts?
B: Yes, high debts at the bank.
A: Can you reduce debts?
B: With a good plan, yes.

From 'Schuld' (guilt/debt). Debts. Die Schulden is always plural when meaning money owed. In German, debt and guilt share the same root. 'Schulden haben' (to have debts) carries a moral weight the English word does not.
loan, credit
From Latin 'creditum' (something entrusted). 'Einen Kredit\naufnehmen' (to take out a loan).

A: Do you need a loan?
B: Yes, for my new office.
A: At which bank will you take out the loan?
B: At the Sparkasse. The conditions are good.

From Latin 'creditum' (something entrusted). A loan, a credit. 'Einen Kredit aufnehmen' (to take out a loan). The Schufa (credit score agency) tracks every German's credit history. Without a good Schufa score, renting an apartment is nearly impossible.
interest rate
Zins (interest) + Satz (rate). 'Zins' comes from Latin 'census.'\nThe ECB in Frankfurt sets the base rate for all of Europe.

A: How high is the interest rate?
B: Currently at 3.5 percent.
A: Has the interest rate risen?
B: Yes, the European Central Bank raised it.

'Zins' (interest) + 'Satz' (rate). Interest rate. 'Zins' comes from Latin 'census.' The European Central Bank in Frankfurt sets the base Zinssatz for all of Europe. Germany's Sparer (savers) watch this number obsessively.
capital
From Latin 'capitalis' (of the head). Both money invested\nand the title of Marx's famous book.

A: Do you have sufficient capital?
B: No, I need more capital for the expansion.
A: Equity or borrowed capital?
B: Preferably equity.

From Latin 'capitalis' (of the head). Capital. Karl Marx wrote 'Das Kapital' in London about Berlin's industrialists. The word means both money invested and the book that changed economic theory forever.
household, budget
Haus (house) + halt (keeping). Both family budget and\nfederal budget use the same word.

A: How does your budget look?
B: My family budget is stable.
A: And the federal budget?
B: That is more complicated. Billions of euros.

'Haus' (house) + 'halt' (keeping). A household or a budget. 'Bundeshaushalt' (federal budget) and 'Familienhaushalt' (family budget) use the same word. Running a country is like running a household, linguistically.
budget (public)
From French 'etat' (state/budget). Borrowed during\nPrussian administration. More elegant than Haushalt.

A: The budget for education has risen.
B: That is good. Education needs money.
A: Who decides about the budget?
B: Parliament. Every year anew.

From French 'etat' (state/budget). A budget, especially a public one. Borrowed from French during Prussian administration. 'Etat' sounds more elegant than 'Haushalt' in political contexts. German newspapers love the French flair.
financial
From French 'financiel,' from Latin 'finis' (end/settlement).\nStress on the last syllable: fi-nan-tsi-ELL.

A: How is your financial situation?
B: Financially I am stable.
A: Being financially independent is the goal.
B: Yes, that is the dream of many Germans.

From French 'financiel,' from Latin 'finis' (end/settlement). Financial. 'Finanziell unabhängig' (financially independent) is the German dream alongside 'Eigenheim' (own home). Stress on the last syllable: fi-nan-tsi-ELL.
economic
From Greek 'oikonomikos' (household management).\nOikos (house) + nomos (law). Laws of the household.

A: The economic situation has improved.
B: Yes, the economic data is positive.
A: What does economic mean exactly?
B: Everything related to the economy.

From Greek 'oikonomikos' (household management). The Greek roots are oikos (house) + nomos (law). Economics literally means 'laws of the household.' The German spelling with oe- preserves the Greek origin.
sustainable, lasting
Nach (after) + haltig (holding). Originally a forestry term:\nonly cut as many trees as can regrow.

A: Is this investment sustainable?
B: Yes, it brings long-term profit.
A: Sustainability is also good for the environment.
B: And for the economy.

'Nach' (after) + 'haltig' (holding). Originally a forestry term: only cut as many trees as can regrow. Germany coined Nachhaltigkeit in 1713. Now it appears everywhere: 'nachhaltige Wirtschaft' (sustainable economy).
profitable, yielding returns
From 'Rente' (return/pension) + '-abel' (able).\nIf something is rentabel, it gives back more than you put in.

A: Is the project profitable?
B: Yes, the return is at 8 percent.
A: From when will it be profitable?
B: Presumably after two years.

From 'Rente' (return/pension) + '-abel' (able). Profitable, yielding returns. 'Eine rentable Investition' (a profitable investment). Related to Rendite (yield). If something is rentabel, it gives back more than you put in.
to manage finances, to economize
From 'Wirt' (host/innkeeper). 'Gut wirtschaften' means\nto manage money well. The innkeeper as original economist.

A: How do you manage your money?
B: I save something every month.
A: Managing money well is an art.
B: Especially when prices rise.

From 'Wirt' (host/innkeeper). To manage finances, to economize. 'Gut wirtschaften' (to manage money well). The innkeeper who runs a good house is the original economist. 'Hauswirtschaft' (home economics) uses the same root.
to finance
From French 'financer.' Non-separable -ieren verb.\nAlways stresses the -ier- syllable.

A: How do you finance the project?
B: Through a loan.
A: Can you also finance it differently?
B: Yes, through investors or equity.

From French 'financer.' To finance. 'Ein Projekt finanzieren' (to finance a project). The -ieren ending marks it as a French/Latin loan verb. These verbs never separate and always stress the -ier- syllable.
to invest
From Latin 'investire' (to clothe). You 'clothe' your money\nin an asset. Non-separable -ieren verb.

A: What do you invest in?
B: In real estate and stocks.
A: Is it worth investing in Germany?
B: Yes, the market is stable.
From Latin 'investire' (to clothe). To invest. You 'clothe' your money in an asset. 'In Aktien investieren' (to invest in stocks). The metaphor: money gets dressed up and goes to work.
annually, yearly
Jahr (year) + -lich. The umlaut pattern:\nTag/täglich, Monat/monatlich, Jahr/jährlich.

A: How often do you have to do the tax return?
B: Annually. Once per year.
A: And the annual costs?
B: The tax advisor calculates those.
'Jahr' (year) + '-lich' (adjective suffix). Annually, yearly. 'Jährliche Steuererklärung' (annual tax return). The umlaut pattern: Tag/täglich, Monat/monatlich, Jahr/jährlich.
What comes in before anything is deducted. The opposite of 'after costs.'
From Latin 'census' via Old High German. The compound describes what a bank charges for borrowed money.
When you owe money and need more, you take out a ...
Since he has debts, Verumius must take out a loan.
Do not worry, Mr. Verumius. By the end of the month we will have your tax return finished. Then everything will look clearer.
Thank you. I thought taxes in Germany would be impossible. But with a good tax advisor, it is doable.
Learn the language of investments and the stock market. Understand how Germans talk about money and markets. Master the financial register.
Learn 20 words about investment and stock market
Understand Aktie, Anlage, Rendite, and Konjunktur
Build your financial vocabulary
A cafe in Mitte. Verumius leafs through the Handelsblatt. Aktie, Konjunktur, Rendite: every line a new world. He picks up a pen and starts underlining words.
stock, share
From Dutch 'actie' (share of a company). Only about 17%\nof Germans own Aktien. Germans prefer Sparbuch.

A: Do you have stocks?
B: Yes, a few DAX stocks.
A: Have the stocks risen?
B: Last week yes, this week no.

From Dutch 'actie' (share of a company). A stock, a share. Germany's main stock index is the DAX (Deutscher Aktienindex). Only about 17% of Germans own Aktien, far less than Americans or British. Germans prefer Sparbuch (savings accounts).
investment, system
An (at) + Lage (laying/position). Covers anything\nyou 'lay down' for future use.

A: Which investment do you recommend?
B: A safe financial investment with good returns.
A: And a solar system?
B: That is a different type of Anlage.
'An' (at) + 'Lage' (laying/position). An investment, or a system/facility. 'Geldanlage' (financial investment), 'Solaranlage' (solar system). The word covers anything you 'lay down' for future use.
investor
Anlage + -er (person). The person who lays down money.\n'Kleinanleger' vs. 'Grossanleger.'

A: Are you an experienced investor?
B: No, I am a small investor.
A: What do small investors usually buy?
B: ETFs and safe funds.
'Anlage' + '-er' (person). An investor. 'Kleinanleger' (small investors) vs. 'Grossanleger' (institutional investors). The person who lays down their money.
stock exchange
From the Belgian family 'van der Beurze' who hosted\nmerchants in medieval Bruges.

A: What is happening at the stock exchange today?
B: Prices have fallen.
A: Why does the stock exchange react like that?
B: Because of the new interest rate decision.

From the Belgian family 'van der Beurze' who hosted merchants in medieval Bruges. A stock exchange. The Frankfurt Börse is Europe's third largest. The word traveled from a Belgian family name to every language.
dividend
From Latin 'dividendum' (that which is to be divided).\nGerman companies pay Dividenden once per year.

A: Does this stock pay a dividend?
B: Yes, once a year.
A: How high is the dividend?
B: Three euros per share.
From Latin 'dividendum' (that which is to be divided). A dividend. The share of profit divided among stockholders. German companies pay Dividenden once per year, unlike quarterly in the US.
return on investment
From Italian 'rendita' (return/yield). Germans calculate\nRendite obsessively before investing.

A: How high is the return?
B: About five percent per year.
A: Is that a good return?
B: For a safe investment, yes.
From Italian 'rendita' (return/yield). Return on investment. Germans calculate Rendite obsessively before investing. The Italian banking term entered German via Venetian traders.
investment (the act)
From Latin 'investire.' Emphasizes the action of investing.\nAnlage emphasizes the asset you hold.

A: This investment is risky.
B: But the return could be high.
A: Every investment has a risk.
B: Without risk, no return.
From Latin 'investire.' An investment (the act). Different nuance from Anlage: Investition emphasizes the action of investing, Anlage emphasizes the asset you hold.
profit
From Latin 'profectus' (progress/gain). Less formal than Gewinn.\n'Profit machen' sounds slightly negative in German.

A: Did the company make a profit?
B: Yes, but 'to achieve profit' sounds better.
A: Why does Profit sound negative?
B: It sounds greedy. Gewinn is neutral.
From Latin 'profectus' (progress/gain). Less formal than Gewinn. 'Profit machen' sounds slightly negative in German, implying greed. 'Gewinn erzielen' is the neutral business term.
corporation, conglomerate
From Latin 'concernere' (to mix/relate). Larger than\nein Unternehmen: a group of companies under one roof.

A: Siemens is a large corporation.
B: Yes, with subsidiaries worldwide.
A: What is the difference from an enterprise?
B: A corporation unites several enterprises.

From Latin 'concernere' (to mix/relate). A corporation, a conglomerate. Siemens, Volkswagen, BASF are German Konzerne. A Konzern is larger than an Unternehmen: it is a group of companies under one roof.
enterprise, company
Unter (under) + nehmen (to take). Germany's Mittelstand\n(mid-sized Unternehmen) employ 60% of all workers.

A: What kind of company is that?
B: A medium-sized enterprise.
A: How many employees does the company have?
B: About 200. Typical Mittelstand.
'Unter' (under) + 'nehmen' (to take). An enterprise, a company. Germany's Mittelstand (mid-sized Unternehmen) employ 60% of all workers. The word means literally 'undertaking.'
competition
Wette (bet) + Bewerb (application/contest). Originally\na bet about who is better.

A: The competition in this sector is tough.
B: Yes, only the best survive.
A: Is free competition always good?
B: Mostly. It lowers prices.
'Wette' (bet) + 'Bewerb' (application/contest). Competition. Originally a bet about who is better. 'Freier Wettbewerb' (free competition) is the foundation of Germany's soziale Marktwirtschaft.
market share
Markt (market) + Anteil (share/portion). The metric\nevery Konzern watches most closely.

A: How large is our market share?
B: About 15 percent.
A: Do we need to increase our market share?
B: Yes, the competition is growing.
'Markt' (market) + 'Anteil' (share/portion). Market share. 'Einen hohen Marktanteil haben' (to have a high market share). The metric every Konzern watches most closely.
demand
Nach (after) + Frage (question/demand). Literally:\nasking after something you want.

A: The demand for electric cars is rising.
B: Yes, supply and demand determine the price.
A: Does demand exceed supply?
B: Currently, yes.
'Nach' (after) + 'Frage' (question/demand). Demand. 'Angebot und Nachfrage' (supply and demand) is Lesson 1 of every economics course. Literally: asking after something you want.
economic cycle, business climate
From Latin 'conjunctura' (joining together).\n'Konjunkturaufschwung' (boom), 'Konjunkturabschwung' (recession).

A: How is the economic climate right now?
B: The economy is recovering slowly.
A: Is an economic boom coming?
B: The experts are optimistic.

From Latin 'conjunctura' (joining together). The economic cycle, the business climate. 'Konjunkturaufschwung' (economic boom), 'Konjunkturabschwung' (recession). Germans track the Konjunktur like weather.
profitable
From French 'profitable.' Synonym of rentabel\nbut more international in tone. Both mean: it makes money.

A: Is the business profitable?
B: Yes, since the second year.
A: What makes it profitable?
B: Low costs and high demand.
From French 'profitable.' Profitable. 'Ein profitables Geschäft' (a profitable business). Synonym of rentabel but more international in tone. Both mean: it makes money.
profit-bringing, lucrative
Gewinn (profit) + bringend (bringing). A compound\nparticiple adjective. German builds these freely.

A: Was the strategy lucrative?
B: Yes, very lucrative.
A: How do you find lucrative projects?
B: Through good market analysis.

'Gewinn' (profit) + 'bringend' (bringing). Profit-bringing, lucrative. 'Eine gewinnbringende Strategie' (a lucrative strategy). A compound participle adjective. German builds these freely: zeitsparend (time-saving), bahnbrechend (groundbreaking).
competitive, able to compete
Wettbewerb (competition) + faehig (capable). At 19 letters,\na typical German compound. The Fugen-s is the glue.

A: Are our prices competitive?
B: Yes, we are cheaper than the competition.
A: Staying competitive is difficult.
B: But necessary.

'Wettbewerb' (competition) + 'faehig' (capable). Competitive, able to compete. 'Wettbewerbsfaehige Preise' (competitive prices). At 19 letters, a typical German compound. The Fugen-s between Wettbewerb and faehig is the glue.
listed on the stock exchange
Börse (stock exchange) + notiert (listed/noted).\nA compound participle adjective with Fugen-n.

A: Is the company listed on the stock exchange?
B: Yes, since 2018.
A: What does publicly listed mean exactly?
B: The stocks are traded on the stock exchange.
'Börse' (stock exchange) + 'notiert' (listed/noted). Listed on the stock exchange. 'Ein börsennotiertes Unternehmen' (a publicly listed company). The Fugen-n connects Börse to notiert.
to invest (money)
An (at) + legen (to lay). Separable: 'Ich lege mein Geld an.'\nOne verb, many meanings depending on context.

A: Where do you invest your money?
B: In funds and real estate.
A: Is it better to invest long-term?
B: Yes, time is the investor's best friend.

'An' (at) + 'legen' (to lay). To invest (money), to create (a garden), to put on (clothes). 'Geld anlegen' (to invest money). Separable: 'Ich lege mein Geld an.' One verb, many meanings depending on what you lay down.
to profit, to benefit
From French 'profiter.' Always with 'von.'\nNon-separable -ieren verb.

A: Who benefits from low interest rates?
B: Everyone who has a loan.
A: Do investors benefit too?
B: Not necessarily. They get less return.

From French 'profiter.' To profit, to benefit. 'Von etwas profitieren' (to profit from something). Always with 'von.' Non-separable -ieren verb. 'Alle profitieren von niedrigen Zinsen' (Everyone benefits from low interest rates).
The Italian banking term for what your money earns when you invest it.
The overall economic cycle that determines business conditions.
The economic cycle determines how supply and demand develop.
'Wettbewerb' is competition. 'Faehig' means capable. Capable of competing.
I just read an entire page of stock market report. Three months ago I would not have understood a word. Now I understand almost everything. Almost.
Learn Futur II for completed future actions and assumptions. Master tax and trade vocabulary. Understand how Germans predict financial outcomes.
Learn Futur II (werden + Partizip II + haben/sein)
Master 18 words about taxes and trade
Understand future predictions in German
Let us look at your balance sheet. By December you will have paid your taxes. Since you export, we can deduct the input tax.
And the inflation? Prices have risen sharply.
The recession will presumably be overcome by spring. Especially since the subsidies are taking effect.
balance sheet
From Italian 'bilancio' (balance). 'Bilanz ziehen' means\nboth a financial and philosophical expression.

A: How does the balance sheet look?
B: The balance sheet shows a profit.
A: When do we take stock?
B: At the end of the fiscal year.

From Italian 'bilancio' (balance). A balance sheet. 'Bilanz ziehen' (to take stock/draw conclusions) is both a financial and a philosophical expression. Every year, companies and individuals 'ziehen Bilanz.'
export
From Latin 'exportare' (to carry out). 'Made in Germany'\ngoods make Germany the world's third-largest exporter.

A: What is Germany's most important export?
B: Cars, machines, and chemicals.
A: How important is export?
B: It drives the economy.
From Latin 'exportare' (to carry out). Export. 'Made in Germany' goods make Germany the world's third-largest exporter. Cars, machines, and chemicals are the top Exportgueter (export goods).
import
From Latin 'importare' (to carry in). Germany imports\nenergy, raw materials, and food.

A: What does Germany import?
B: A lot of energy and raw materials.
A: Has importing risen?
B: Yes, especially for energy.
From Latin 'importare' (to carry in). Import. Germany imports energy, raw materials, and food. The Handelsbilanz (trade balance) between Export and Import has been positive for decades.
inflation
From Latin 'inflare' (to blow up). Germans have a deep\ncultural fear of Inflation because of 1923.

A: How high is inflation?
B: Currently at about three percent.
A: Is that a lot?
B: For German standards, yes.

From Latin 'inflare' (to blow up). Inflation. Germans have a deep cultural fear of Inflation because of 1923, when a loaf of bread cost billions of Marks. The Bundesbank was designed to prevent it ever again.
recession
From Latin 'recessio' (retreat). Two consecutive quarters\nof negative growth. The economy retreats like a tide.

A: Are we in a recession?
B: Technically yes. Two quarters negative.
A: How long does a recession last?
B: It varies. Sometimes months, sometimes years.
From Latin 'recessio' (retreat). Recession. Two consecutive quarters of negative growth. Germans call it also 'Konjunkturflaute' (economic doldrums). The economy retreats like a tide going out.
subsidy
From Latin 'subventio' (assistance). Germany spends\nbillions on Subventionen for agriculture, energy, industry.

A: Does the sector receive subsidies?
B: Yes, from the state.
A: Why are there subsidies?
B: To support certain sectors.

From Latin 'subventio' (assistance). A subsidy. The German government spends billions on Subventionen for agriculture, energy, and industry. 'Subventionen abbauen' (to reduce subsidies) is a perpetual political debate.
industry, sector
From French 'branche' (branch). Each branch of the\neconomic tree is a Branche.

A: What industry do you work in?
B: In the IT sector.
A: Has the sector grown?
B: Yes, enormously in recent years.
From French 'branche' (branch). An industry, a sector. 'In welcher Branche arbeiten Sie?' (What industry do you work in?). Each branch of the economic tree is a Branche.
raw material
Roh (raw) + Stoff (material). Germany is poor in Rohstoffe\nbut rich in engineering.

A: Which raw materials does Germany import?
B: Oil, gas, and rare earths.
A: Is there a shortage of raw materials?
B: In some areas, yes.

'Roh' (raw) + 'Stoff' (material). A raw material. Germany is poor in Rohstoffe but rich in engineering. 'Rohstoffmangel' (resource shortage) shaped Germany's industrial strategy: import raw, export finished.
tax rate
Steuer (tax) + Satz (rate). Germany's top\nEinkommensteuersatz is 45%.

A: How high is your tax rate?
B: At my income, about 35 percent.
A: Does the tax rate rise with income?
B: Yes, that is the progressive system.
'Steuer' (tax) + 'Satz' (rate). Tax rate. Germany's top Einkommensteuersatz is 45%. The progressive system means the Steuersatz rises with income. Every German knows their Steuersatz.
wage tax
Lohn (wage) + Steuer (tax). Deducted directly from\nyour paycheck. Germans never see this money.

A: What is wage tax?
B: The tax on your salary.
A: Who pays the wage tax?
B: Your employer deducts it directly.
'Lohn' (wage) + 'Steuer' (tax). Wage tax, income tax on employment. Die Lohnsteuer is deducted directly from your paycheck by your employer. Germans never see this money. It vanishes before payday.
liable to tax
Steuer (tax) + pflichtig (obligated). Everyone above\nthe Grundfreibetrag is steuerpflichtig. No exceptions.

A: Am I liable to tax in Germany?
B: Yes, everyone with income is liable to tax.
A: From which amount?
B: From the basic allowance, about 11,000 euros.
'Steuer' (tax) + 'pflichtig' (obligated). Liable to tax. Everyone earning above the Grundfreibetrag (basic tax-free allowance) in Germany is steuerpflichtig. No exceptions.
fiscal
From Latin 'fiscalis' (of the treasury). A formal,\ntechnical word found in economic reports.

A: What fiscal measures does the government plan?
B: Tax cuts and more spending.
A: Are fiscal reforms necessary?
B: Yes, the budget must be consolidated.

From Latin 'fiscalis' (of the treasury). Fiscal. 'Fiskalische Maßnahmen' (fiscal measures). The German Fiskus (treasury) collects taxes. A formal, technical word found in economic reports and policy papers.
related to the business cycle
Konjunktur + -ell. Everything that rises and falls\nwith the economic tide is konjunkturell.

A: Are these cyclical fluctuations?
B: Yes, the economy fluctuates cyclically.
A: How long do business cycle phases last?
B: Usually five to ten years.

'Konjunktur' + '-ell' (adjective suffix). Related to the business cycle. 'Konjunkturelle Schwankungen' (cyclical fluctuations). Everything that rises and falls with the economic tide is konjunkturell.
to export
From Latin 'exportare.' Non-separable -ieren verb.\nSame word across European languages.

A: What does Germany export the most?
B: Cars and machines.
A: Where do we export to?
B: To the whole world. China, USA, Europe.
From Latin 'exportare.' To export. 'Deutschland exportiert Autos in die ganze Welt' (Germany exports cars to the whole world). Non-separable -ieren verb. Same word across European languages.
to import
From Latin 'importare.' The mirror verb of exportieren.\nBoth are non-separable -ieren verbs.

A: Does Germany import a lot of energy?
B: Yes, oil and gas are imported.
A: Can we import less?
B: With renewable energy, yes.
From Latin 'importare.' To import. 'Rohstoffe importieren' (to import raw materials). The mirror verb of exportieren. Both are non-separable -ieren verbs.
to tax
Be- + steuern (to tax/steer). The be- prefix makes it\ntransitive. Not separable.

A: How is income taxed?
B: Progressively. The more, the higher the rate.
A: Are pensions also taxed?
B: Yes, partially.
'Be-' + 'steuern' (to tax/steer). To tax. 'Einkommen besteuern' (to tax income). The be- prefix makes it transitive. Not separable. 'Hoch besteuert' (heavily taxed) describes most things in Germany.
to save, to cut costs
Ein (in) + sparen (to save). Separable: 'Wir sparen 20% ein.'\nMore specific than 'sparen': reducing from a previous level.

A: Can we cut costs?
B: Yes, on materials we save 15%.
A: Where else?
B: On energy costs.

'Ein' (in) + 'sparen' (to save). To save, to cut costs. 'Kosten einsparen' (to cut costs). Separable: 'Wir sparen 20% ein.' More specific than just 'sparen': einsparen implies reducing from a previous level.
presumably, expected
Voraus (ahead) + sichtlich (visibly). A hedging word\nthat financial analysts use constantly.

A: When will the economy grow?
B: Presumably in the next quarter.
A: Is that certain?
B: No, voraussichtlich means 'probably.'

'Voraus' (ahead) + 'sichtlich' (visibly). Presumably, expected. 'Voraussichtlich wird die Wirtschaft wachsen' (The economy is expected to grow). A hedging word that financial analysts use constantly. Never commit, always qualify.

German has a tense for actions that will be completed in the future. It is formed with werden plus Partizip II plus haben or sein.

Examples:
- 'Bis Dezember werde ich die Steuern bezahlt haben.'
- 'Die Rezession wird bis Frühjahr überwunden sein.'

The Futur II has two uses:
- Completed future describes what will be finished by a specific point: 'Bis morgen werde ich alles erledigt haben.'
- Assumption about the past expresses probability: 'Er wird wohl schon gegangen sein.' (He has probably already left.)

The choice between haben and sein follows the same rule as the Perfekt. Most verbs take haben (bezahlt haben, geschrieben haben). Movement and change-of-state verbs take sein (gegangen sein, gestiegen sein).

The Futur II is formed with werden + Partizip II + haben/sein. It describes an action that will be completed by a future point, or expresses a probability judgment about the past.

The auxiliary (haben or sein) follows the same rules as the Perfekt:
- haben: most transitive verbs, reflexive verbs
- sein: movement verbs (gehen, kommen, fliegen), change-of-state verbs (sterben, wachsen, steigen), sein and bleiben

Word order: werden is conjugated and in position 2. The Partizip II and haben/sein go to the end.
- Hauptsatz: Er wird die Steuern bezahlt haben.
- Nebensatz: ..., weil er die Steuern bezahlt haben wird.

With 'wohl' or 'wahrscheinlich,' Futur II expresses assumption:
- Er wird wohl krank gewesen sein. (He was probably sick.)
werden + Partizip II + haben. An action that will be completed by a specific point in time.
to fall, to decrease, to sink
Strong verb. Sinken/sank/gesunken.\nTakes 'sein'. Used for prices, values, temperatures.

A: Have prices fallen?
B: Yes, inflation has fallen significantly.
A: How far has it fallen?
B: By two percent in the last quarter.

Strong verb: sinken/sank/gesunken. Takes 'sein' because it describes a change of state. 'Die Zinsen sinken' (Interest rates are falling), 'die Temperaturen sinken' (temperatures drop). The opposite is 'steigen' (to rise). Essential in economic and financial German.
Futur II of a verb of motion/change. This verb takes 'sein', not 'haben'. Which Partizip II fits 'falling prices'?
Inflation will have fallen by spring.
By December I will have submitted the tax return. The tax advisor promised that. And I have taken note of it.
Master advanced causal connectors beyond weil and denn. Analyze economic cause-and-effect relationships. Build complex argumentative sentences.
Learn causal clauses with da, zumal, umso mehr als, insofern als
Master 18 words about macroeconomics and business cycles
Translate economic relationships into complex sentences
insolvency
From Latin 'insolventia' (inability to pay). 'Insolvenz\nanmelden' is a formal legal step.

A: Did the company file for insolvency?
B: Yes, last week.
A: What happens during an insolvency?
B: An insolvency administrator takes over.

From Latin 'insolventia' (inability to pay). Insolvency. 'Insolvenz anmelden' (to file for insolvency) is a formal legal step. Germany reformed its Insolvenzrecht (insolvency law) to give debtors a second chance after six years.
bankruptcy
From Italian 'banca rotta' (broken bench). Medieval money\nchangers worked at benches. Broken bench = bankruptcy.

A: The company is bankrupt.
B: Bankrupt? That is the end.
A: What is the difference from insolvency?
B: Bankruptcy is final. Insolvency can be survived.
From Italian 'banca rotta' (broken bench). Medieval Italian money changers worked at benches. When they could not pay, the bench was broken. The broken bench became bankruptcy.
growth
From 'wachsen' (to grow) + '-tum.' The -tum ending\nalways creates neuter nouns: Eigentum, Altertum.

A: How high is economic growth?
B: About 1.5 percent.
A: Is that enough growth?
B: For Germany, that is solid.

From 'wachsen' (to grow) + '-tum' (state/condition). Growth. 'Wirtschaftswachstum' (economic growth) is the metric politicians celebrate. The -tum ending always creates neuter nouns: Eigentum, Altertum, Wachstum.
upswing, boom
Auf (up) + Schwung (swing/momentum). The recovery\nphase of the business cycle. The economy swings upward.

A: Is an upswing coming soon?
B: The experts say yes.
A: How do you recognize an upswing?
B: More orders, fewer unemployed.
'Auf' (up) + 'Schwung' (swing/momentum). An upswing, a boom. 'Wirtschaftlicher Aufschwung' describes the recovery phase of the business cycle. The economy swings upward.
stimulus package
Konjunktur + Paket. Governments deploy these during\nrecessions. Germany's 2020 package was 130 billion euros.

A: What is a stimulus package?
B: Money from the state to boost the economy.
A: Do stimulus packages work?
B: Usually yes, but they cost a lot.

'Konjunktur' (economic cycle) + 'Paket' (package). A stimulus package. Governments deploy these during recessions. Germany's 2020 Konjunkturpaket was 130 billion euros. A package to restart the economic engine.
deficit
From Latin 'deficit' (it is lacking). Germany's Schuldenbremse\nin the constitution limits the federal Defizit.

A: Does the budget have a deficit?
B: Yes, spending exceeds revenue.
A: How large is the deficit?
B: Several billion euros.

From Latin 'deficit' (it is lacking). A deficit. 'Haushaltsdefizit' (budget deficit). Germany's Schuldenbremse (debt brake) in the constitution limits the federal Defizit. No other country constitutionally bans deficits.
trade balance
Handel (trade) + Bilanz (balance). The difference between\nexports and imports. Germany's is typically positive.

A: How does the trade balance look?
B: Positive. We export more than we import.
A: Is a positive trade balance always good?
B: Usually, but there is criticism.
'Handel' (trade) + 'Bilanz' (balance). Trade balance. The difference between exports and imports. Germany's Handelsbilanz is typically positive: more goes out than comes in.
national economy, macroeconomics
Volk (people) + Wirtschaft (economy). 'VWL' is the study\nof entire economies. Contrast: BWL studies companies.

A: What is Volkswirtschaft?
B: The economy of an entire country.
A: And Betriebswirtschaft?
B: The economics of individual companies.

'Volk' (people) + 'Wirtschaft' (economy). National economy, macroeconomics. 'Volkswirtschaftslehre' (VWL) is the study of entire economies. Contrast: Betriebswirtschaft (BWL) studies individual companies.
globalization
Global + -isierung (process suffix). The -ung ending\nmakes it feminine.

A: How does globalization affect Germany?
B: Through more trade and competition.
A: Is there criticism of globalization?
B: Yes, especially from trade unions.

'Global' + '-isierung' (process suffix). Globalization. Germany is one of the most globalized economies on Earth. The -ung ending makes it feminine. 'Globalisierungskritiker' (critics of globalization) are loud in Germany.
operating at a deficit
Defizit + -aer. The -aer ending comes from French\nand always signals an adjective describing a state.

A: Is the budget in deficit?
B: Yes, we spend more than we take in.
A: How long can you run a deficit?
B: Not forever. Eventually the debts come.
'Defizit' + '-aer' (adjective suffix). Operating at a deficit. 'Ein defizitaerer Haushalt' (a deficit budget). The -aer ending comes from French and always signals an adjective describing a state.
relating to the market economy
Marktwirtschaft (market economy) + -lich. Germany calls\nits system 'soziale Marktwirtschaft.'

A: Is Germany a market economy?
B: Yes, but with social security.
A: What are market economy principles?
B: Free competition and private ownership.

'Marktwirtschaft' (market economy) + '-lich.' Relating to the market economy. 'Marktwirtschaftliche Prinzipien' (market economy principles). Germany calls its system 'soziale Marktwirtschaft' (social market economy): capitalism with a safety net.
macroeconomic
Volkswirtschaft + -lich. GDP, inflation, unemployment\nare volkswirtschaftliche Indikatoren.

A: What is macroeconomic data?
B: GDP, inflation, unemployment rate.
A: Why is it important?
B: It shows the state of the economy.

'Volkswirtschaft' + '-lich.' Macroeconomic. 'Volkswirtschaftliche Daten' (macroeconomic data). The adjectival form of the national economy. GDP, inflation, unemployment are volkswirtschaftliche Indikatoren.
relating to business economics
Betriebswirtschaft (business administration) + -lich.\nThe micro-level counterpart to volkswirtschaftlich.

A: What are business metrics?
B: Revenue, profit, cost per unit.
A: And macroeconomic ones?
B: GDP, trade balance, inflation.
'Betriebswirtschaft' (business administration) + '-lich.' Relating to business economics. 'Betriebswirtschaftliche Kennzahlen' (business metrics). The micro-level counterpart to volkswirtschaftlich.
to compete
From Latin 'concurrere' (to run together).\nNon-separable -ieren verb. Originally: running the same race.

A: Does your company compete with Siemens?
B: Yes, we compete for the same clients.
A: Is it hard to compete with large corporations?
B: Yes, but we are more flexible.
From Latin 'concurrere' (to run together). To compete. 'Mit jemandem konkurrieren' (to compete with someone). Non-separable -ieren verb. Originally: running the same race.
to restructure, to renovate
From Latin 'sanare' (to heal). Healing a sick business.\nAlso used for buildings.

A: Does the company need to be restructured?
B: Yes, the finances are critical.
A: How do you restructure a company?
B: Cut costs, reduce debts, reposition.

From Latin 'sanare' (to heal). To restructure, to renovate. 'Ein Unternehmen sanieren' (to restructure a company). Healing a sick business. Also used for buildings: 'ein Gebaede sanieren' (to renovate a building).
to consolidate
From Latin 'consolidare' (to make firm). Making something\nsolid that was shaky. Standard political-economic vocabulary.

A: Do we need to consolidate the budget?
B: Yes, the debts are too high.
A: How do you consolidate a budget?
B: Spend less, earn more.
From Latin 'consolidare' (to make firm). To consolidate. 'Den Haushalt konsolidieren' (to consolidate the budget). Making something solid that was shaky. Standard political-economic vocabulary.
to speculate
From Latin 'speculari' (to observe). Originally:\nwatching from a watchtower. Now: bets on the future.

A: Do you speculate on the stock market?
B: No, that is too risky for me.
A: Many speculate with cryptocurrencies.
B: Yes, but speculation is not investing.
From Latin 'speculari' (to observe). To speculate. 'An der Börse spekulieren' (to speculate on the stock market). Originally: watching from a watchtower. Now: making bets on the future.
approximately, by estimation
Schätzung (estimate) + -weise (manner). A hedging adverb\nlike voraussichtlich. German financial language loves qualifiers.

A: How many jobs will be lost?
B: Approximately 10,000.
A: Is that certain?
B: No, schätzungsweise means 'approximately.'

'Schätzung' (estimate) + '-weise' (manner). Approximately, by estimation. 'Schätzungsweise 50 Milliarden Euro' (approximately 50 billion euros). A hedging adverb like voraussichtlich. German financial language loves qualifiers.

At B2 level you need more than weil and denn for cause and effect.

New connectors, sorted by formality:
- da (since/because): 'Da die Wirtschaft wächst, steigen die Einnahmen.' Formal, written. Verb at the end.
- zumal (especially since): 'Die Investition lohnt sich, zumal die Zinsen niedrig sind.' Emphasizes the reason.
- umso mehr als (all the more since): 'Das ist wichtig, umso mehr als die Konkurrenz wächst.'
- insofern als (insofar as): 'Das stimmt, insofern als die Daten korrekt sind.' Academic.
- in Anbetracht der Tatsache, dass: Bureaucratic. Very formal.
- nämlich (you see, because): 'Er investiert, er hat nämlich Kapital.' AFTER the verb.
- und zwar (specifically): 'Wir sparen, und zwar 20%.' Specifies.

All create subordinate clauses with verb at the end, except nämlich and und zwar.

Da vs. weil: Both mean 'because/since,' but da is more formal and common in written German. Da-clauses usually come first in the sentence. Weil-clauses usually come second.

Zumal: 'Especially since.' Adds weight to a reason. Always comes after the main clause: '..., zumal die Zinsen niedrig sind.'

Umso mehr als: 'All the more since.' Strongest emphasis on the cause. Written, formal.

Insofern als: 'Insofar as.' Limits the scope of a claim. Academic precision.

In Anbetracht der Tatsache, dass: 'In view of the fact that.' Seven words to say 'because.' Maximum bureaucratic formality.

Nämlich: Post-verbal. Main clause word order. 'Er spart; er hat nämlich Schulden.' Explains after the fact.

Und zwar: Specifies. 'Wir investieren, und zwar in Aktien.' Introduces the detail.
'Zumal' strengthens the statement. Not simply 'weil' (because), but 'especially because.'
The formal connector for 'since/as.' Two letters. Typical of elevated written German.
Since exports are rising, revenue is growing.
werden + Partizip II + haben. Something that will be completed by a specific date.
to recover, to recuperate
Reflexive verb. Er/sie erholt sich.\nUsed for people resting and economies recovering.

A: Is the economy recovering?
B: Yes, the economy is recovering slowly.
A: When will it have fully recovered?
B: By the end of the year, if the subsidies work.

Reflexive verb: ich erhole mich, du erholst dich, er erholt sich. Used both for people recovering from illness or stress and for markets and economies recovering. 'Die Erholung' is the noun. 'Der Erholungsort' (holiday resort) comes from the same root.
Formal causal clause + Futur II. Gap 1: written connector (two letters). Gap 2: Partizip II of 'sich erholen.'
Since the government gives subsidies, the sector will have recovered by 2025.
Fixed noun-verb combinations are the secret of formal German. The tax advisor finishes his consultation, and Mia talks about Elterngeld. Learn six collocations every professional knows.
Master fixed noun-verb combinations in formal context
Understand Germany's social benefits system
Practice all three grammar constructions together
So, Mr. Verumius. We need to make a decision. Are you considering the small business regulation, or do you want the full sales tax?
I need to consider that. What is available to me?
Later that afternoon. A Berlin park in autumn. Mia and Verumius walk together. The conversation turns personal: Mia is thinking about parental benefit.
I need to make a decision. Parental benefit or career. Or both. Germany makes both available, but you also have to accept that everything changes.
privatization
Feminine noun. Privat + -isierung.\n'Die Privatisierung der Deutschen Post.'

A: The privatization was controversial.
B: Which privatization do you mean?
A: That of the Deutsche Bahn.
B: Yes, it is still debated today.
After reunification in 1990, the Treuhandanstalt privatized 8,000 East German state companies. The largest privatization program in history reshaped Germany's economy overnight.
gross domestic product (GDP)
Neuter noun. Brutto + Inland + Produkt.\nAbbreviated: BIP. Das BIP Deutschlands.

A: How high is the gross domestic product?
B: GDP is over 4 trillion euros.
A: That makes Germany the largest economy in Europe.
B: And the fourth largest worldwide.
At 25 letters, Bruttoinlandsprodukt is a workout. Everyone says BIP (pronounced like 'bipp'). Germany has the fourth-largest BIP in the world after the USA, China, and Japan.
social benefit
Feminine noun. Sozial + Leistung (benefit).\nPlural: Sozialleistungen.

A: What social benefits exist in Germany?
B: Child benefit, parental benefit, unemployment benefit, housing benefit.
A: That is an extensive system.
B: Yes, social benefits cost over 30% of GDP.
Kindergeld, Elterngeld, Arbeitslosengeld, Wohngeld, Bürgergeld: Germany's social safety net is built from dozens of Sozialleistungen. The system costs more than any military budget.
net salary, take-home pay
Neuter noun. Netto + Gehalt.\n'Mein Nettogehalt ist deutlich weniger als mein Bruttolohn.'

A: What is your net salary?
B: About 2,800 euros.
A: And your gross wage?
B: 4,500. The difference is painful.
What arrives in your bank account. Typically 55-65% of your Bruttolohn. The gap between Brutto and Netto shocks every newcomer to Germany. Taxes plus social contributions devour the rest.
gross wage
Masculine noun. Brutto + Lohn.\n'Der Bruttolohn steht im Arbeitsvertrag.'

A: My gross wage is 5,000 euros.
B: And how much arrives net?
A: Approximately 3,100.
B: Almost 40% deductions. Welcome to Germany.
The number you negotiate but never see. Before Lohnsteuer, Sozialabgaben, and Krankenversicherung eat their share. The Bruttolohn is a promise; the Nettogehalt is reality.
retail trade
Masculine noun. Einzel + Handel.\n'Im Einzelhandel arbeiten' (to work in retail).

A: Retail trade has changed.
B: In what way?
A: More online trade, fewer physical stores.
B: Nevertheless, retail remains important.
Germany's Einzelhandel includes ALDI, LIDL, and REWE, all global retail giants born in Germany. Despite the rise of online shopping, German city centers still fight to keep their Einzelhandel alive.
wholesale trade
Masculine noun. Gross + Handel.\n'Der Großhandel liefert an den Einzelhandel.'

A: In wholesale, you buy in large quantities.
B: Who is wholesale intended for?
A: For businesses, not for end customers.
B: So business-to-business.
Buying and selling in bulk. Metro AG is Germany's largest Großhandel company. You need a business card to shop there. Selling to businesses, not consumers.
foreign trade
Masculine noun. Aussen + Handel.\n'Der Aussenhandel ist stark.'

A: Germany's foreign trade is one of the strongest worldwide.
B: What does Germany mainly export?
A: Cars, machines, and chemicals.
B: And what is imported?
A: Energy and raw materials.
Germany's Aussenhandel is one of the strongest in the world. The Aussenhandelsbilanz (foreign trade balance) has been positive for decades: more goes out than comes in.
to make a decision
Fixed NVV. Entscheidung + treffen.\nFormal register. Not 'machen.'

A: We need to make a decision.
B: About which topic?
A: An important business decision.
B: Then let us make it together.
You do not 'machen' a decision in formal German. You 'treffen' it, as if aiming at the right target. Entscheidung always pairs with treffen in business and legal contexts.
to take into consideration
Fixed NVV. Betracht + ziehen.\n'Alle Optionen in Betracht ziehen.'

A: Have you considered all options?
B: Yes, we have considered everything.
A: Also the cheaper alternative?
B: We are considering that as well.
You pull something into your field of view. Formal, essential in business and legal writing. The Partizip II form 'in Betracht gezogen' appears in every board meeting.
to be available
Fixed NVV. Verfügung + stehen.\n'Ich stehe Ihnen jederzeit zur Verfügung.'

A: What funds are available?
B: 50,000 euros are available.
A: Are advisors also available?
B: Yes, I am available to you at any time.
'Ich stehe Ihnen jederzeit zur Verfügung' is the gold standard of formal German politeness. Literally: I stand at your disposal. The perfect closing line for business emails.
to exert influence on
Fixed NVV. Einfluss + nehmen + auf.\n'Die Regierung nimmt Einfluss auf die Wirtschaft.'

A: Who exerts influence on the decision?
B: The shareholders exert great influence.
A: Can the government also exert influence?
B: Yes, it exerts influence on the market through laws.
You take influence upon something. More deliberate than simply 'beeinflussen.' In political and economic contexts, Einfluss nehmen implies strategic, purposeful action.
to accept (a drawback)
Fixed NVV. Kauf + nehmen.\n'Ich nehme die Nachteile in Kauf.'

A: You have to accept certain risks.
B: Which risks, for example?
A: Losses. You have to accept those.
B: No risk, no reward.
You take something negative into the deal. You accept a drawback as part of a larger bargain. 'Ich nehme die Nachteile in Kauf' means: I know the downsides, and I accept them anyway.
to make a contribution
Fixed NVV. Beitrag + leisten.\n'Jeder kann einen Beitrag leisten.'

A: Everyone should make a contribution.
B: What kind of contribution do you mean?
A: A financial contribution to the project.
B: I am happy to make my contribution.
You perform a contribution. Formal and positive. The civic-minded way to say 'help out.' Politicians love this phrase: 'Jeder muss seinen Beitrag leisten.'
to deduct (from taxes), to sell, to remove
Separable verb. Ab + setzen.\n'Kosten von der Steuer absetzen.'

A: Can I deduct that from taxes?
B: Yes, you deduct business expenses.
A: Also the home office?
B: You deduct up to 1,260 euros per year.
Three distinct meanings depending on context: deduct from taxes, sell goods, or remove someone from office. 'Von der Steuer absetzen' is every German freelancer's favorite phrase.
to implement, to generate revenue
Separable verb. Um + setzen.\n'Einen Plan umsetzen' (to implement a plan).

A: How do we implement the plan?
B: Step by step.
A: The company generates 2 million in revenue annually.
B: Impressive. Well implemented.
Separable verb with two meanings: implement a plan or generate revenue. 'Millionen umsetzen' means generating millions in turnover. The verb of action in business contexts.
to privatize
Non-separable -ieren verb.\n'Staatliche Betriebe privatisieren.'

A: Should the Deutsche Bahn be privatized?
B: That has been debated for a long time.
A: England privatized its railway.
B: With mixed results.
The opposite of verstaatlichen (to nationalize). Germany has done both at different points in history. The Treuhandanstalt privatized an entire country's economy after 1990.
monthly
Monat + -lich. Adverb and adjective.\n'Monatliche Kosten' (monthly costs).

A: The monthly costs are 500 euros.
B: Is it debited monthly?
A: Yes, the debit occurs monthly.
B: At the beginning or end of the month?
The pattern: Tag/täglich, Woche/wöchentlich, Monat/monatlich, Jahr/jährlich. German loves regular time adjectives. Monatlich is the rhythm of rent, insurance, and subscriptions.
Fixed noun-verb collocations use specific verb partners. Formal German avoids 'machen' for this type of action noun.
A fixed noun-verb collocation for careful evaluation. The noun names the mental process of weighing options.
All options must be taken into consideration.
Count the fixed noun-verb pairs. Both availability and decision-making are formal collocations.
Futur II of the NVV for careful consideration. The structure: werden + Partizip II + haben.
By March he will have taken the investment into consideration.
I will claim the parental benefit. And Lukas will finance his music school. We accept the changes. That is how it works here.
Consider, be available, accept the drawbacks. These are not words. They are tools. Without them, you cannot get by in Germany.
Lukas proudly shows his sound workshop. Verumius reflects on the German economic system in the evening. All three grammar constructions are practiced in complex combinations.
Practice all three grammar constructions in combination
Master advanced business vocabulary
Use noun-verb combinations naturally

Kreuzberg, a small backyard studio. A hand-painted sign: 'Klangwerkstatt Kreuzberg.' Inside: two guitars, a keyboard, children's drawings on the wall. Lukas stands in the middle, grinning. This is his dream.
I made a decision. The music school is my thing. Since I teach children, I also have to do bookkeeping. Imagine that!
You realized a dream. By next year you will have established yourself.
I still need to build up equity. But especially since demand is high, it will work out.
bookkeeping
Feminine noun. Buch + Fuehrung (leading).\n'Die Buchführung muss korrekt sein.'

A: Do you do the bookkeeping yourself?
B: Yes, I handle the bookkeeping.
A: Bookkeeping is mandatory for every company.
B: That is why I bought software.
Every German business must keep Buecher (books). The Finanzamt can demand to see them at any time. Germans helped develop double-entry bookkeeping alongside the Italians.
equity, own capital
Neuter noun. Eigen + Kapital.\n'Das Eigenkapital der Firma beträgt 100.000 Euro.'

A: How high is your equity?
B: About 30,000 euros so far.
A: That is a good start.
B: Building up equity takes time.
The money you own free and clear. Contrast with Fremdkapital (borrowed money). A healthy company needs both. The ratio determines whether a bank will give you a Kredit.
borrowed capital, debt
Neuter noun. Fremd + Kapital.\n'Das Unternehmen hat zu viel Fremdkapital.'

A: How much borrowed capital does the company have?
B: 200,000 euros from the bank.
A: The ratio of equity to borrowed capital is important.
B: Yes, too much borrowed capital is risky.
Money from outside: bank loans, bonds, credit. The ratio of Eigenkapital to Fremdkapital determines a company's financial health. German banks look at this ratio before every Kredit.
total costs
Feminine, always plural. Gesamt + Kosten.\n'Die Gesamtkosten übersteigen das Budget.'

A: How high are the total costs of the project?
B: The total costs are 500,000 euros.
A: That exceeds our budget.
B: Then we need to lower the total costs.

Always plural. 'Die Gesamtkosten übersteigen das Budget' is every project manager's nightmare. Breaking Gesamtkosten into Einzelkosten (individual costs) is the first step to understanding where money goes.
annual report
Masculine noun. Geschäft + Bericht.\n'Den Geschäftsbericht lesen.'

A: Did you read the annual report?
B: Yes, it shows rising revenues.
A: The annual report is publicly accessible.
B: Every listed company must publish one.
Every listed company in Germany must publish one. It contains Bilanz, Gewinn- und Verlustrechnung, and an outlook. Investors read Geschäftsberichte like detectives looking for clues.
cost center
Feminine noun. Kosten + Stelle.\n'Buchen Sie das auf Kostenstelle 4711.'

A: Which cost center does that go to?
B: Cost center 301, Marketing.
A: Every department has its own cost center.
B: That way you can assign expenses precisely.
A department or unit where costs are tracked. German accounting precision at its finest. The number 4711 is famous as both a cologne brand and a classic example Kostenstelle number.
reserve, savings set aside
Feminine noun. Rueck + Lage.\n'Finanzielle Rücklagen bilden.'

A: Do you have reserves for emergencies?
B: Yes, three months' salary as a reserve.
A: That is wise. Reserves are important.
B: Without reserves, a crisis can be fatal.
Money laid back for emergencies. The German savings mentality in one word. Financial advisors recommend three to six Monatsgehälter as Ruecklage. Better safe than insolvent.
balance of payments
Feminine noun. Zahlung + Bilanz.\n'Die Zahlungsbilanz eines Landes.'

A: The balance of payments shows all international transactions.
B: Is it positive or negative?
A: Germany's balance of payments is positive.
B: That means more money flows in than out.
All financial transactions between one country and the rest of the world. Broader than Handelsbilanz: includes services, investments, and transfers. Germany's has been positive for decades.
monopoly
Neuter noun. From Greek monos + polein.\n'Ein Monopol auf etwas haben.'

A: Does Deutsche Bahn have a monopoly?
B: Practically yes, a quasi-monopoly.
A: The cartel office fights against monopolies.
B: Competition is better for consumers.
Germany's Kartellamt (cartel office) fights Monopole. Deutsche Bahn is often called a Quasi-Monopol on rail. Same word internationally from the Greek for 'alone selling.'
fund (investment)
Masculine noun. From French fonds.\nPronounced 'fo' (silent -nds).

A: Which fund do you invest in?
B: In an ETF fund.
A: Funds are more popular than individual stocks.
B: Yes, they spread the risk better.
Pronounced 'fo' with a silent -nds (French style). Germany's most popular investment form: ETF-Fonds. The French banking term entered German and kept its French pronunciation.
solvent, able to pay debts
From Latin solvere (to pay).\n'Das Unternehmen ist solvent.'

A: Is the company still solvent?
B: Yes, the company is solvent.
A: Solvent companies find loans more easily.
B: In contrast to insolvent ones.
The opposite of insolvent. When a company can pay its debts, it is solvent. The adjective that banks want to hear before giving a Kredit.
industry-specific
Branche + spezifisch. 19 letters.\n'Branchenspezifische Regelungen.'

A: There are industry-specific regulations.
B: For which industries?
A: Every industry has industry-specific rules.
B: The music industry too?
At 19 letters, a typical B2 compound adjective. Every sector has its own branchenspezifische Regelungen. German bureaucracy customizes rules per Branche.
to make use of, to claim
Fixed NVV. Anspruch + nehmen.\n'Elterngeld in Anspruch nehmen.'

A: Would you like to claim parental benefit?
B: Yes, I am claiming it.
A: That is your good right.
B: How long can you claim it?
You take a claim. Formal, bureaucratic, but essential for accessing German benefits. 'Elterngeld in Anspruch nehmen' is the key phrase for new parents navigating the system.
to refer to
Fixed NVV. Bezug + nehmen + auf.\n'Ich nehme Bezug auf Ihr Schreiben.'

A: I refer to our conversation.
B: Which conversation do you mean?
A: Referring to the last meeting...
B: Ah, the meeting from last Tuesday.
Standard opening in formal correspondence. 'Ich nehme Bezug auf Ihr Schreiben vom 5. Mai' connects your letter to theirs. The cross-reference formula of German business writing.
to take note of, to acknowledge
Fixed NVV. Kenntnis + nehmen.\n'Ich nehme das zur Kenntnis.'

A: We take note of your complaint.
B: And what happens then?
A: Please take note that the deadline is expiring.
B: Noted.
You take something into your knowledge. Can be neutral or cold: 'Ich nehme das zur Kenntnis' sometimes means 'I acknowledge that, but I do not agree.' Tone reveals everything.
to call into question, to challenge
Fixed NVV. Frage + stellen.\n'Die Ergebnisse in Frage stellen.'

A: May one call the results into question?
B: Of course. Everything may be questioned.
A: He is questioning the entire strategy.
B: That is his right.
You place something into the realm of questions. Intellectual, critical, essential for B2-level discourse. Not aggressive, but assertive: demanding evidence.
to merge, to fuse
Non-separable -ieren verb.\n'Zwei Konzerne fusionieren.'

A: The two corporations will merge.
B: When does the merger take place?
A: They merge on January 1.
B: That will change the market.
From Latin fusio (melting). When companies melt into one. Germany's Kartellamt must approve every major Fusion to prevent Monopole from forming.
to draw up a balance sheet, to take stock
Non-separable -ieren verb.\n'Am Jahresende bilanzieren wir.'

A: At year's end we must draw up the balance.
B: What does the balance show?
A: We have a positive balance.
B: Profit or loss? Positive sounds good.
Both literal (accounting) and figurative (life reflection). 'Am Jahresende Bilanz ziehen' means both crunching numbers and reflecting on what was achieved.
Identify each structural layer: what opens the sentence, what tense describes an action completed by a deadline, and what secondary connector adds emphasis.
A fixed NVV for challenging validity. The noun describes an act of inquiry, not of weighing or purchasing.
The company calls the results into question.
Count the fixed noun-verb pairs. Referencing, taking a position, and questioning are all formal collocations.
Futur II of 'Bilanz ziehen' (to take stock) plus a formal causal connector.
By the end of the year he will have taken stock, since the fiscal year is ending.
You know, Verumius, bookkeeping is not so bad. You just have to take note of every receipt. And build enough reserves. Then the shop runs.
Lukas, the musician, is explaining bookkeeping to me. Berlin changes everyone.
Hard quiz mode. Prove your mastery of financial German. Test all three grammar constructions. Show you can build complex economic arguments.
Prove mastery of financial German
Test all three grammar constructions
Show ability to build complex economic arguments
State library, Potsdamer Platz. Verumius sits among economics textbooks and newspaper articles. Today he will test what he has learned. Numbers, data, facts. No more guessing.
employer
Masculine noun. Arbeit + Geber.\nDer Arbeitgeber gibt Arbeit.

A: Who is your employer?
B: My employer is a corporation in Frankfurt.
A: Employers have many obligations.
B: Yes, social insurance, vacation, dismissal protection.
The one who gives work. In German law, Arbeitgeber have extensive obligations: social insurance, vacation days, sick pay. The 'giver' gives much more than just work.
employee
Masculine noun. Arbeit + Nehmer.\nDer Arbeitnehmer nimmt die Arbeit an.

A: How many employees does the company have?
B: Over 500 employees.
A: Employee rights are strong in Germany.
B: Yes, dismissal protection is comprehensive.
The one who takes work. The pair Arbeitgeber/Arbeitnehmer is perfectly symmetrical: one gives, one takes. German labor law strongly protects the Nehmer.
supply, offer
Neuter noun. An + Gebot (bid).\n'Angebot und Nachfrage' (supply and demand).

A: Supply exceeds demand.
B: Then prices fall.
A: We are making you an offer.
B: I will review the offer.
The counterpart to Nachfrage in every economics textbook. 'Angebot und Nachfrage' is lesson one. Also: 'ein Angebot machen' (to make an offer). One word, two key meanings.
crisis
Feminine noun. From Greek krisis (decision).\n'Eine Wirtschaftskrise' (economic crisis).

A: The financial crisis hit many companies.
B: How did Germany survive the crisis?
A: With stimulus packages and short-time work.
B: Every crisis is also an opportunity.
The Greek root means the moment of decision, not the disaster itself. 'Wirtschaftskrise' (economic crisis), 'Finanzkrise' (financial crisis). In German, the Krise is where you must choose.
to prove, to demonstrate
Fixed NVV. Beweis + stellen.\n'Seine Kompetenz unter Beweis stellen.'

A: He demonstrated his abilities.
B: How did he do that?
A: Through excellent results.
B: Competence must be proven, not just claimed.
You place something under proof. More formal than beweisen. In business, you 'stellen Ihre Kompetenz unter Beweis' through results, not words.
to come into force/effect
Fixed NVV. Kraft + treten.\n'Das Gesetz tritt am 1. Januar in Kraft.'

A: When does the law come into force?
B: It comes into force on July 1.
A: From then on, the new rules apply.
B: We still have time until it takes effect.
A law steps into force. Legal German at its most precise. 'Inkrafttreten' (the coming into force) is the noun form. Every German law has an exact date of Inkrafttreten.
to take measures
Fixed NVV. Maßnahmen + ergreifen.\n'Die Regierung muss Maßnahmen ergreifen.'

A: What measures did the government take?
B: Tax relief and subsidies.
A: Are these measures sufficient?
B: We need to take further measures.
You seize the measures. Politicians' favorite phrase during any Krise. 'Sofortmaßnahmen ergreifen' (to take immediate measures) signals urgency and action.
to take stock, to sum up
Fixed NVV. Bilanz + ziehen.\n'Am Jahresende Bilanz ziehen.'

A: Let us take stock.
B: Yes, it is time to take stock.
A: The assessment is positive.
B: Good. Next year we will take stock again.
You draw a balance line. Both financial (accounting) and philosophical (life reflection). Every year, companies and individuals 'ziehen Bilanz' over what was achieved.
to file for bankruptcy
Separable. Konkurs + anmelden.\n'Die Firma hat Konkurs angemeldet.'

A: Did the company file for bankruptcy?
B: Yes, it filed for bankruptcy last week.
A: How many employees are affected?
B: Over 200 will lose their positions.
You register your inability to pay. The formal legal step when a business fails. Separable: 'Er meldet Konkurs an.' The German Insolvenzrecht gives debtors a second chance after six years.
to take responsibility
Fixed NVV. Verantwortung + übernehmen.\n'Jeder muss Verantwortung übernehmen.'

A: Who takes responsibility?
B: The managing director takes responsibility.
A: Taking responsibility is courageous.
B: Yes, but necessary.
You take responsibility over onto yourself. Germany's civic motto. In politics and business, 'Verantwortung übernehmen' is the difference between a leader and a figurehead.
to play a role, to matter
Fixed NVV. Rolle + spielen.\n'Geld spielt eine grosse Rolle.'

A: Does price play a role?
B: Yes, price plays a decisive role.
A: And quality?
B: Both play an important role.
Same metaphor as English: life is theater. Every factor plays its part. 'Eine grosse Rolle spielen' (to play a major role) and 'keine Rolle spielen' (to not matter) are everyday expressions.
to take into account, to bear in mind
Fixed NVV. Rechnung + tragen.\n'Den Risiken Rechnung tragen.'

A: We must take the risks into account.
B: How do we account for that?
A: Through better planning.
B: One must take the changed market into account.
You carry the account. Formal, used in reports and speeches. Takes dative: 'den Risiken Rechnung tragen.' The expression of due diligence in German business.
to nationalize
Non-separable. Ver- + Staat + -lichen.\nOpposite of privatisieren.

A: Should energy supply be nationalized?
B: That is a political question.
A: Some countries nationalized their banks.
B: In a crisis the state nationalizes, afterwards it privatizes.
The opposite of privatisieren. Germany has nationalized and privatized in cycles. During the 2008 financial crisis, several European countries temporarily verstaatlichten banks.
to amortize, to recoup
Non-separable -ieren verb. Reflexive: sich amortisieren.\n'Die Investition amortisiert sich in drei Jahren.'

A: When does the investment pay for itself?
B: In about three years.
A: That is an acceptable timeframe.
B: Yes, after that it generates profit.
From French 'amortir' (to extinguish/pay off). Reflexive when meaning 'to pay off': 'Die Investition amortisiert sich.' The moment of Amortisation is when costs equal returns.
to liquidate, to wind up
Non-separable -ieren verb.\n'Ein Unternehmen liquidieren.'

A: The company is being liquidated.
B: What happens to the assets?
A: Everything is sold and distributed.
B: Liquidating is the end of a company.
Making the accounts liquid: turning everything into cash. From Latin 'liquidare' (to make clear/settle). In business: winding up a company. The final step after insolvency.
to outsource
From English. Pronounced with English phonetics.\n'Dienstleistungen outsourcen.'

A: Should we outsource the IT?
B: That saves costs.
A: Many companies outsource their accounting.
B: The German expression would be 'auslagern.'
German borrowed it wholesale from English. Pronounced with English phonetics. Some purists prefer 'auslagern' but outsourcen dominates business jargon. A Denglisch classic.
to budget
Non-separable -ieren verb.\n'Kosten budgetieren' (to budget costs).

A: Have you budgeted the costs?
B: Yes, everything is budgeted.
A: Good budgeting is half the battle.
B: Without a budget, no overview.
The French word 'budget' came from Old French 'bougette' (small bag). Planning what fits in your financial bag. The -ieren ending makes it a standard German business verb.
quarterly
Quartal + -weise. Every three months.\n'Quartalweise Berichte.'

A: The report is published quarterly.
B: So four times a year?
A: Exactly. Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4.
B: The quarterly figures are important for investors.
German business runs on quarterly cycles. Quartalszahlen (quarterly figures) move stock prices. The -weise suffix creates manner adverbs: monatlich/monatweise, quartalweise, stückweise.
Identify three distinct layers: the causal opener, a formal noun-verb pairing for action, and the tense form that places an action as finished before a given point in time.
The NVV for a law becoming legally active. Think about what a law gains when it takes effect: power, strength.
The new law comes into force on January 1.
Futur II combined with 'wohl' signals not a plan but a judgment about what has probably already happened.
Formal causal clause + Futur II. 'Since he took responsibility, the firm will have recovered.'
Since he took responsibility, the firm will have recovered by 2026.
A precise, academic causal connector. It limits the scope of the statement.
The reflexive verb for an investment that earns back its own cost over time. Common in financial planning contexts.
The investment will pay for itself in three years.
A formal NVV for giving proper weight to something. The noun is related to calculation or accounting.
Numbers, data, facts. I have proven that I command the language of economics. At least on paper. Tomorrow comes the practice.
Verumius sits at his desk in the evening and draws a mind map of the German economic system. All NVV, causal connectors, and Futur II in complex arguments. The system finally makes sense.

Use all NVV, causal connectors, and Futur II in complex arguments
Understand the German economic model
Verumius's apartment, evening. He sits at his desk, drawing a mind-map. Taxes, pension, health insurance, child benefit, parental benefit: all connected by arrows. The system makes sense now.
I have taken stock. Since I understand the tax system and can make use of the social benefits, I feel I have arrived in Germany.

He pins the mind-map next to his corkboard of German life documents. The collection grows: registration, rental contract, running club membership, work contract. And now: an understanding of the system.
Social market economy. You pay a lot, but you get a lot back too. Since I understand that now, I can finally join the conversation. Not just living in Germany, but understanding how it works.
to express
Fixed NVV. Ausdruck + bringen.\n'Seine Meinung zum Ausdruck bringen.'

A: How does one express one's opinion?
B: In a formal statement.
A: He expressed his concerns.
B: That was courageous and important.
You bring something to expression. More formal than 'ausdrücken.' Used in speeches and formal writing. The NVV of diplomatic and official communication.
to consider, to contemplate
Fixed NVV. Erwägung + ziehen.\nMore careful than 'in Betracht ziehen.'

A: We are considering a change of location.
B: For what reason?
A: Since rental costs are rising.
B: That should be carefully considered.
More thoughtful than 'in Betracht ziehen.' You pull something into deliberation. 'Wir ziehen einen Wechsel in Erwägung' implies careful, slow weighing of pros and cons.
to generate (profit/revenue)
Non-separable. Er- + wirtschaften.\n'Gewinne erwirtschaften.'

A: How much profit did the company generate?
B: 2 million euros.
A: That was hard-earned.
B: Yes, the success was not given as a gift.
The er- prefix means achieving a result through effort. Managing your economy until you produce results. 'Gewinne erwirtschaften' is the formal business term for making money.
to depreciate, to write off
Separable verb. Ab + schreiben.\n'Einen Verlust abschreiben.'

A: Can the equipment be depreciated?
B: Yes, over five years.
A: How much do we write off this year?
B: 20% of the acquisition costs.
Two meanings: depreciate in accounting and write off as a loss. Also means 'to copy' in school. Context decides: tax office or classroom. Separable: 'Wir schreiben 10.000 Euro ab.'
to distribute (dividends)
Separable verb. Aus + schütten.\n'Dividenden ausschütten.'

A: When are dividends distributed?
B: In May after the annual meeting.
A: How much is distributed?
B: 3 euros per share.
You pour profits out to the shareholders. Separable: 'Die Firma schüttet Dividenden aus.' German companies typically ausschütten once a year, unlike quarterly in the US.
to subsidize
Non-separable -ieren verb.\n'Die Landwirtschaft subventionieren.'

A: Should the state subsidize agriculture?
B: That has been debated for years.
A: Many sectors are subsidized.
B: The question is: for how much longer?
Government money flows to support sectors that would otherwise struggle. 'Subventionen abbauen' (to reduce subsidies) is a perpetual political debate in Germany and the EU.
to repay (a debt), to extinguish
Regular verb. From Middle High German.\n'Schulden tilgen' (to repay debts).

A: When will the debts be repaid?
B: In ten years.
A: We repay 500 euros monthly.
B: After ten years the debt is extinguished.
You destroy the debt by paying it off. From Middle High German 'tilgen' meaning to destroy or erase. The word carries the weight of elimination. Every final payment is an act of tilgen.
since, as (causal conjunction)
Formal written causal connector. Verb-final.\n'Da er investiert, wächst sein Kapital.'

A: Since the economy grows, revenues rise.
B: That sounds logical.
A: Since he pays taxes, he is entitled to benefits.
B: Cause and effect.
As a causal conjunction: 'since/as.' Formal, written. Places the cause before the effect. Different from temporal 'da' (there/then). Context decides.
especially since
Adds emphasis to a reason. Stronger than da.\n'Die Investition lohnt sich, zumal die Zinsen niedrig sind.'

A: The project is worthwhile, especially since we receive subsidies.
B: Especially since demand is rising.
A: Let us invest, especially since the risk is low.
B: Agreed. Especially since we have reserves.
'Zu' + 'mal' (time/instance). Adds weight to a reason. Not just because, but especially because. Stronger than 'da,' more elegant than 'besonders weil.'
all the more since
Emphasizes that a cause makes a claim even more true.\n'Das ist wichtig, umso mehr als die Konkurrenz wächst.'

A: We must invest, all the more since competition is strong.
B: That is true, all the more since we are lagging behind.
A: Quality is important, all the more since the market is growing.
B: Yes, all the more since customers are becoming more demanding.
Emphasizes that the cause makes the statement even more true. Academic and journalistic register. The triple-word connector that intensifies any argument.
namely, you see (explanatory)
Placed AFTER the verb in main clause.\n'Er investiert nicht; er hat nämlich kein Kapital.'

A: He cannot invest. He has no capital, you see.
B: That is precisely the problem.
A: We must save. The costs are too high, you see.
B: Namely since last quarter.
From 'Name' (name). Explains the reason after the fact. Placed AFTER the verb in the main clause. Conversational but also formal. 'Er hat nämlich kein Geld' provides the reason casually.
namely, specifically
Specifies or clarifies the preceding statement.\n'Wir sparen, und zwar 20%.'

A: We must cut costs, namely immediately.
B: And by what percentage exactly?
A: By 15%, namely in all departments.
B: That is ambitious.
Introduces the precise detail. Punchy and efficient. 'Und zwar' says: I will now tell you exactly what I mean. The clarifier of German argumentation.
after all, ultimately
From schliessen (to close). Provides concluding reason.\n'Er arbeitet hart; schließlich hat er Schulden.'

A: He works hard. After all, he has debts.
B: After all, he has to feed his family.
A: Investing is worthwhile. After all, the market is growing.
B: After all, growth is the goal.
Provides the concluding reason. 'After all' implies the listener should have known. Slightly argumentative. From 'schliessen' (to close): closing the argument.
insofar as
Academic, precise, limiting connector.\n'Das stimmt, insofern als die Daten korrekt sind.'

A: That is profitable, insofar as demand remains stable.
B: Insofar as the data is correct, yes.
A: Insofar as we remain solvent, we can expand.
B: An important qualification.
The academic, precise causal connector that limits a claim. 'This is true, but only to the extent that...' Essential for nuanced B2-level argumentation.
in view of the fact that
Most formal causal connector. Bureaucratic.\n'In Anbetracht der Tatsache, dass die Kosten steigen...'

A: In view of the fact that costs are rising, we must save.
B: That sounds very formal.
A: In view of the fact that jobs are affected...
B: ...we must act carefully.
Seven words to say 'because.' The most formal causal connector in German. Bureaucratic, political, and legal. Used when every other word must carry weight.
namely, specifically (formal)
More formal than 'und zwar.' Written register.\n'Einige Branchen, namentlich die Automobilindustrie.'

A: Some sectors profit, namely the tech sector.
B: And namely the retail sector?
A: Namely the export-oriented companies.
B: That is specific enough.
From 'Name' + '-lich' (suffix) + '-t' (adverb). More formal than 'und zwar.' Specifies who or what is meant. Written register for academic and legal texts.
for, because (main clause connector)
Main-clause word order after denn.\n'Er investiert, denn er hat Kapital.'

A: I invest, because the interest rates are low.
B: That is wise, because the return is right.
A: We save, because the crisis is not over yet.
B: Caution is important, because the situation is uncertain.
Coordinating conjunction: main-clause word order follows (unlike weil). Already known from earlier levels, but now part of the full causal connector toolkit alongside da, zumal, nämlich, und zwar.

Germany's economy is neither pure capitalism nor socialism. It is a 'social market economy': free markets with strong social safety nets.

The government sets the rules (Ordnungspolitik) but does not run companies.
Employees have co-determination rights (Mitbestimmungsrechte).
Social insurance covers health, pension, unemployment, care, and accidents.

Ludwig Erhard designed this system after 1945. It produced the Wirtschaftswunder and remains Germany's economic identity.

Ordnungspolitik: the state sets the framework (competition law, cartel office, consumer protection) but does not intervene in individual markets.

Prozesspolitik: direct interventions such as stimulus packages or subsidies. Normally only in times of crisis.

The ideal: Ordnungspolitik as the rule, Prozesspolitik as the exception. The social market economy lives from this balance.
Count: one complex causal opener, one NVV, one emphasizing causal closing.
The minister gives voice to something. The NVV means to make an internal feeling externally visible. Not 'Beweis' (proof) or 'Betracht' (consideration).
The minister expresses his concerns.
werden + Partizip II + haben. A completed future action with a deadline.
to deploy, to begin, to set in
Separable verb: ein + setzen. Two main meanings:\n1. to deploy/use; 2. to begin/set in (of processes).

A: When does the upswing begin?
B: The upswing begins as soon as inflation falls.
A: Will new markets be deployed?
B: Yes, new resources will be deployed.

Separable: 'Der Aufschwung setzt ein' (The upswing begins/sets in). In economics: processes 'einsetzen' when they start. Resources and workers are 'eingesetzt' (deployed). Two-meaning verb: natural processes begin, while humans deploy tools or people.
Three structural layers in one sentence: a formal causal opener, a completed-future verb form, and an emphatic secondary causal connector.
Since the economy has recovered, the upswing will have begun by 2027, especially since new markets are emerging.
